section.name: Native UDP Request And Receiver
section.p: BreakTest includes UDP support in its standard distribution. No JMeter Plugins common I/O or common JMeter library is required. Add UDP Request or UDP Receiver from a test-plan node's Sampler menu.

ul.li: Hostname or IP address and Destination port select the remote endpoint.
Response timeout is the maximum receive wait in milliseconds. Zero waits indefinitely.

Wait for response receives one datagram after sending. The operating system reassembles IP fragments before BreakTest receives it, but separate UDP datagrams are not combined. Clear the option for fire-and-forget traffic.
Local bind address and Local bind port control the source interface and port. A blank port uses an ephemeral port.
Close socket after request closes an unnamed socket after each sample. Named sockets stay open for reuse.
Allow unexpected disconnect treats an ICMP port-unreachable result as a successful sample.

section.p: Choose a friendly Payload format from the sampler. The default Text (UTF-8) option accepts normal text. Hexadecimal bytes accepts pairs of hexadecimal digits and exposes responses as lowercase hexadecimal. Single-byte text (ISO-8859-1) maps each request character directly to one byte for legacy protocols. The Custom codec class option accepts a public no-argument class that implements org.apache.jmeter.protocol.udp.sampler.UDPTrafficCodec.

A non-empty Socket ID shares one connected socket within the current virtual user. Different virtual users always get separate sockets, even when they use the same Socket ID. This prevents responses from being consumed by the wrong user. The first UDP Request for an ID must provide the remote endpoint and optional local bind settings. Later UDP Requests can provide only the same Socket ID; they use the endpoint and binding of the already-open socket. Repeating the endpoint fields remains useful when a sampler should also be able to create the socket, and BreakTest verifies that repeated settings match. Use the same ID in UDP Receiver to collect a later or unsolicited datagram. Operations on a named socket are serialized. Reusing an ID with different remote or local bind settings fails explicitly, and named sockets close when the virtual user finishes.

UDP Receiver can treat a timeout as either a successful no-content sample or a failed request. The receive buffer is reused between samples and defaults to the maximum UDP payload size. It can be reduced with the udp.receive_buffer_size property. A response exceeding a reduced limit fails with response code 413 instead of being silently truncated.

BreakTest rejects payloads larger than the protocol maximum before sending them: 65,507 bytes over IPv4 or 65,527 bytes over IPv6. Requests larger than a typical Ethernet UDP payload produce a one-time log warning because IP fragmentation makes loss more likely. Change the warning threshold with udp.fragmentation_warning_size, or set it to zero to disable the warning.

IP fragmentation is transparent to the sampler: the operating system either delivers one fully reassembled UDP datagram or discards it when reassembly fails, in which case the sampler times out. Multiple application-level UDP datagrams are independent messages and require protocol-specific sequence or length fields; BreakTest does not concatenate them automatically.
